nativeRange.habitat: Shallow coral reefs, generally found at depths of 1–20 meters

diet.notes: In the wild, feeds primarily on coral polyps, worms, tunicates, and small crustaceans. In captivity, tends to accept mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, finely chopped seafood (clam, mussel, shrimp, squid), and frozen meaty preparations. Has been documented being difficult to wean off coral polyps, and juveniles under approximately 7 cm often struggle to adapt to prepared foods. Feeding several times daily is generally recommended, particularly during the acclimation period.

summary: The Pakistan Butterflyfish (Chaetodon collare) is a striking Indo-Pacific species with a rich brown body, white head stripe, and vivid red tail. Generally considered an advanced-level fish due to acclimation challenges and dietary needs; best kept singly or as a bonded pair in a FOWLR setup.
careNotes:
The Pakistan Butterflyfish is generally considered one of the more challenging butterflyfishes to maintain in captivity. Acclimatization to prepared foods tends to be difficult, particularly in juveniles smaller than approximately 7 cm; acquiring medium-sized or larger specimens (>7 cm) is commonly recommended to improve acclimation success. The species has been reported to have high early mortality rates in captivity despite careful preparation.

In the wild, the diet consists primarily of coral polyps, making this species not reef-safe — it has been documented nipping at stony corals, soft corals, tubeworms, anemones, and other sessile invertebrates including Tridacna clams. It is best suited to a fish-only-with-live-rock (FOWLR) aquarium.

Adults tend to occur singly or in pairs on coral reefs. The species is generally peaceful toward non-similar fish but may show aggression toward other butterflyfish or similar-shaped species unless introduced simultaneously. When keeping a pair, both fish should be introduced to the aquarium at the same time.

This species has been documented forming monogamous pair bonds in the wild, consistent with many Chaetodon butterflyfishes. Excellent water quality, strong water movement, and open swimming space are generally advised. The tank should provide ample rockwork for shelter in addition to open swimming areas.

Temperature range: sources vary from 22–29°C across aquarium hobby and wild ecology data. An averaged range of approximately 23–27°C is suggested here; some sources note a narrower 23–25°C for captive care while FishBase records wild-preferred temperatures up to ~29°C.

Tank size: sources disagree between 55 gallons (208L) and 125 gallons (473L). Given the species' active swimming behavior, need for open space, and typical recommendation to keep as a pair, a minimum of 400L is suggested here for a single specimen; pairs should be housed in at least 500L.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity has not been reported to occur successfully. The species is documented as oviparous with monogamous pairing during reproduction in the wild.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy, adult size (18 cm TL), temperature range (wild-preferred 25.3–29.2°C), and native range. FishBase temperature is higher than hobby sources, suggesting the averaged 23–27°C represents a reasonable captive care range.

additional.name: ReefApp Encyclopedia
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size (18 cm), minimum tank volume (400L), temperament, diet, and reef-unsafe status. Notes high captive mortality and recommends acquiring specimens >7 cm.

additional.name: AquariumDomain
additional.notes: Additional temperature cross-check (72–82°F = 22.2–27.8°C), tank size reference (55 gal minimum stated for single specimens), diet and temperament confirmation.

additional.name: Fishkeeper.co.uk
additional.notes: Confirmed temperature range (23–27°C), adult size (16 cm), pH, salinity, and not-reef-safe status. Notes juveniles under 7.5 cm struggle to acclimate.

fish.breedingNotes: No successful captive breeding has been reported. Monogamous pairing during reproduction is documented in the wild; the species is oviparous.
